facts.text: Movies from the 2000s through the mid-2020s were shaped by franchises, CGI advances, streaming, and global box-office growth.
The Lord of the Rings trilogy, released from 2001 to 2003, proved that large-scale fantasy could dominate both critics' lists and the box office.
The Dark Knight (2008) helped elevate comic-book films in awards conversations and popular criticism.
Avatar (2009) became a landmark for 3D exhibition and motion-capture spectacle.
The Marvel Cinematic Universe, launched with Iron Man in 2008, became the defining interconnected film franchise of the era.
Harry Potter released eight films between 2001 and 2011, turning a book franchise into a global movie phenomenon.
Christopher Nolan's Inception (2010) proved that original high-concept blockbusters could still become major hits.
Mad Max: Fury Road (2015) was praised for combining practical stunt work with modern visual effects.
The 2010s saw animation thrive through studios like Pixar, Disney Animation, DreamWorks, Illumination, and Sony Pictures Animation.
Get Out (2017) showed that horror could also function as sharp social commentary and awards-worthy filmmaking.
Parasite (2019) became the first non-English-language film to win Best Picture at the Oscars.
Streaming platforms changed release patterns, funding models, and how audiences discovered prestige films and documentaries.
The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted theatrical releases worldwide and accelerated experiments with hybrid and streaming-first premieres.
Top Gun: Maverick (2022) became a major theatrical event and reminded studios how powerful the big-screen experience could still be.
Barbenheimer became the nickname for the simultaneous July 2023 release of Barbie and Oppenheimer, turning moviegoing into a pop-culture event.
Oppenheimer won Best Picture for films released in 2023 and showed audiences still embraced serious adult dramas on a huge scale.
Dune: Part One and Dune: Part Two helped reestablish big-budget science fiction as both visually ambitious and critically respected.
The rise of IP-driven filmmaking meant more movies were based on comics, games, books, toys, and legacy sequels than ever before.
Global box-office success increasingly depended on international audiences, especially in Europe, Latin America, and Asia.
From 2000 to 2026, digital filmmaking became the norm, but many top directors still fought to preserve film stock and theatrical presentation.
